content.full_text:
Farn PoLtrrc,q,L Pnncttcns CouvtIssloN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(9t6) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
May 15,2001
Karin D. Troedsson, Deputy Town Attorney
Town of Yountville
6550 Yount Street
Yountville, C494599
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-077
Dear Ms. Troedsson:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Mary Lou
Holt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").'
QUESTIONS
1. May Mayor Holt participate in the review of the floodwall project?
2. May Mayor Holt participate in the review of the plans to remodel the community
hal1?
3. May Mayor Holt participate in ongoing management decisions for the community
hall?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in a decision to approve the
floodwall project. Since she has a conflict of interest in this decision, she may not
participate in a review of this project.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 I 09- I 8996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 2
2. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in a decision to remodel and
expand the community hall and may not participate in that decision.
3. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in any decision relating to the
ur. *d manag".Lnt of the community hall, unless the "public generally" exception
appties. You have not provided suffrcient information for us to reach a conclusion on this
issue.
FACTS
The Town of Yountville is a small town in the Napa Valley. The town
encompasses an area ofjust 1.56 square miles and, as of January l, 2000, had a
population of 3,768.
The town is considering the erection of a floodwall that would protect two mobile
home parks located at the end of the town from future flooding. Mayor Holt's principal
residence is within 500 feet of the floodwall. However, her properly is not in the flood
zone and will not be benefited by flood protection from the wall. The Yountville town
engineer has estimated that approximately 70 percent of the homeowners in the town also
will not receive a flood protection benefit from this project.
Additionally, the town council will soon be reviewing a project to remodel and
expand the community hall, a public building. There are also ongoing issues of use and
-unug".n.nt of this public building that come before the council from time to time. The
mayor's principal residence is within 500 feet of the corner of this property.
Approximat ely 2.5 percent of all homeowners are within the same distance of the
community center and may be affected by increased trafiic, parking, or noise as a result
of the remodeling and expansion of the community hall. If you consider access to the
facilities, the effect on Mayor Holt would be indistinguishable from 100 percent of the
homeowners or property owners within Yountville, since she will not receive any
preferential access to this public facility.
You have specifically asked whether the "public generally" rule would apply to
any of these decisions.
ANALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her offrcial position to influence a governmental decision in
which the offrcial has a financial interest.


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 3
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 of the Act
requires analysis of the following questions:2
Is Mayor Holt a o'public official" for purposes of the Act?
As Mayor of Yountville, Ms. Holt is a "member, officer, employee, or consultant
of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, is strbject to the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)
Is Mayor Holt making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public ofiicial "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her ofiice or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1') A
prrUti. offrcial "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review,
the oflicial negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision before his
or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, ofiicer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section
87 1 00; Regulation 187 02.3.)
Mayor Holt will "make a governmental decision" if she votes on the floodwall
project, community hall remodeling, or management decisions involving the community
hall. Additionally, if she engages in any of the actions detailed above with regard to
these decisions, she will be "participating in making" or "influencing" a governmental
decision.
What is Mayor Holt's economic interest - the possible source of a conflict of
interest?
Section 87103 provides thatapublic offrcial has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
2 These questions are based on the Act's conflict of interest analysis provided at Regulation
18700(b). The Commission document "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Oveniew" explains the steps of
this analysis and is enclosed for your information


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 4
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
. 
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
hai a direct or indirect investment 3 of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703 1(a)); or inwhich he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation I 8703. t (b));
. 
I public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
1s703.2);
. 
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
. 
I public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregateto $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(e); Regulation I 8703.4);
. 
I public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5)''
You have inquired about, and provided facts regarding, only Mayor Holt's
principal residence. For purposes of this letter, we assume that Mayor Holt has no other
iconomic interests relevant to the governmental decision about which you inquire'
Provided Mayor Holt has an investment of $2,000 or more in her principal
residence, she has an economic interest in this residential property for purposes of the
Act.
' An indirect investment or interest means any investrnent or interest owned by the spouse of an
offrcial or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public ofEcial, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the offrcial's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or benefrcially a l0-percent interest or gleater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family' is defined
at Section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
o please bear in mind that the Commission has recently completed a comprehensive review of its
conflict of interest regulations, and has adopted numerous amendments to them, which were effective on
February 1, 2001. This letter is based on the new amendments.


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 5
Is Mayor Holt's economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
govern mental decision ?
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if that real property is the subject of the
governmental decision, or if any part of that real property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) ofthe real property that is the subject ofthe
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a).) You have stated that Mayor Holt's
residence is within 500 feet of the proposed floodwall. Additionally, Mayor Holt's
residence is located within 500 feet of the community hall. Therefore, Mayor Holt's
property is directly involved in each governmental decision about which you have
inquired.
Materiality standard - what financial impact on your economic interest from the
decision is considered material?
When a public official's real property interest is directly involved in a
governmental decision, the materiality standards provided in Regulation 18705.2(a) are
applicable. (Regulation 18704.2(b)(1) ) Regulation 18705.2(a) provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property (other than a leasehold)
which is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material,
absent proof that will rebut this presumption, as discussed below.
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will result in the
materiality standard for your economic interest being met?
Floodwall
. 
Generally, it is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable that a public offrcial's
property, located within 500 feet of property which is the subject of a governmental
decision, will experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision.
(Regulation 15705.2(a)(1).) This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial
effect on the real property. (Regulation 18705.2(aXt) ) You have presented information
from the town engineer which states that Mayor Holt's property will not receive any
flood protection benefit from the erection of the floodwall. However, the burden is on
the mayor to provide sufficient facts to rebut the presumption that other consequences,
particularly negative effects, of the floodwall's construction could financially affect her
property. In absence of such a showing, we presume it is reasonably foreseeable that
Mayor Holt's property will experience a material financial affect as a result of the
floodwall decision. As a result, Mayor Holt has a conflict of interest in this decision.
Community Hall Remodel and Expansion
It is likewise presumed that it is reasonably foreseeable that Mayor Holt's
property, located within 500 feet of the community hall, will experience a material


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 6
financial effect as a result of the decision to approve the remodel and expansion of the
hall. As previously stated, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonabiy foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on
the real property. However, the facts you have provided do not rebut the presumption.
To the ctntrary, you have identified impacts from this decision such as increased traflic,
parking diffrculties, and noise which may financially affect the mayor's property.
-Consequently, Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in the decision to
approve the remodel and expansion of the community hall.
Ongoing Management Decisions
Absent facts showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating
to the use and management of the community hall will have a material financial effect on
the mayor's property, the mayor is also presumed to have a conflict of interest with
regard to these decisions.
Does the "Public Generally" exception apply?
Under the "public generally" exception, an oflicial may still participate ih a
decision if the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interest is
indistinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18707(a).) This "public generally" exception is codified in Regulations
18707-18707.9. Pursuant to these provisions, if a "significant segment" of the
jurisdiction is affected by the governmental decision in substantially the same manner as
it would affect the public official, then the offrcial may participate in the decision.
The Commission's regulations contain specific "public generally" provisions
which apply to decisions in small jurisdictions with a population of 25,000 or less.
(Regulation LS7O7 .3.) The jurisdiction of Yountville meets this condition.
However, application of this "small jurisdiction" regulation also requires that:
"(3) The real property that serves as the public offrcial's
principal residence is more than 500 feet from the
boundaries of the property which is the subject of the
decision." (Regulation 18707.3(a)(3).)
Since Mayor Holt's residence is located within 500 feet of the subject of each deciston
about which you seek advice, the "public generally" rule for small jurisdictions does not
apply, and the general rule must be applied to each decision on which you seek advice.
For decisions affecting a public offrcial's real property interest, the general rule
provided by Regulation 18707.I provides that a"significant segment" is comprised of:


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 7
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the offrcial represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the offtcial's agency."
(Regulation r87 07 .1 (b)( 1)(B).)
Because the entire population of Mayor Holt's jurisdiction is 3,768, subdivision
(ii) cannot apply. Therefore, under subdivision (i) we examine whether ten percent or
more of all property owners or all homeowners in Mayor Holt's jurisdiction will be
affected by each decision in substantially the same manner as Mayor Holt.
Floodwall
Without additional information regarding each consequence of the decision to
construct the floodwall, we are unable to determine whether ten percent or more of all the
property owners will be affected by the floodwall decision in substantially the same
manner as Mayor Holt. To make this determination, you must identify all measurable
effects from the decision to construct the floodwall. Such effects must include both
positive and negative consequences resulting from the construction of the floodwall, and
those homeowners who will be affected must be identified. Next, each effect on the
homeowners must be measured in such a way that the magnitude of the effect can be
determined. If, based on an analysis of this information, you conclude that 10 percent or
more of the homeowners will be affected in substantially the same manner as the mayor,
then the "public generally" rule will apply, and Mayor Holt will be allowed to participate
in the floodwall decision.
Community Hall Remodel and Expansion
Approximately 2.5 percent of homeowners are located within 500 feet of the
community hall. However, 10 percent of the homeowners must be affected in
"substantially the same mannel' as the mayor for the exception to apply.
You have also indicated that the effect of access by the mayor to the community
center would be indistinguishable from its effect on a significant segment of the public
generally since the mayor will not receive any preferential access to this public facility.
While this may be the case, access is only one factor which may affect the mayor's
interest in her real property and is not dispositive as to whether the "public generally"
rule applies. Therefore, our conclusion does not change; the mayor is presumed to have a
conflict of interest in the decision to approve the remodel and expansion of the
community center.
Ongoing Management Decisions
You have not provided enough information relating to the particular management
decisions before the mayor for us to make an assessment as to whether the public


File No. A-01-077
Page No. 8
generally exception would apply to these decisions. Specifically, we require additional
information regarding the possible financial effects such decisions could have on an
economic interest of Mayor Holt and on a "significant segment" of the public. If the
public generally exception does not apply, then Mayor Holt is presumed to have a
conflict and cannot participate in these decisions.
We conclude our analysis here since the facts you have provided do not suggest
that the final step of the conflict of interest analysis, the "legally required participation"
exception, is applicable to Mayor Holt's situation.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
CI
Natalie B
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

principal residence is more than 500 feet from the
boundaries of the property which is the subject of the
decision." (Regulation 18707.3(a)(3).)

the district the offrcial represents; or

jurisdiction of the offtcial's agency."

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Mayor Holt participate in the review of the floodwall project?
2. May Mayor Holt participate in the review of the plans to remodel the community
hal1?
3. May Mayor Holt participate in ongoing management decisions for the community
hall?

CONCLUSION: 1. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in a decision to approve the
floodwall project. Since she has a conflict of interest in this decision, she may not
participate in a review of this project.
2. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in a decision to remodel and
expand the community hall and may not participate in that decision.
3. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in any decision relating to the
ur. *d manag".Lnt of the community hall, unless the "public generally" exception
appties. You have not provided suffrcient information for us to reach a conclusion on this
issue.

Troedsson, Deputy Town Attorney Town of Yountville 6550 Yount Street Yountville, C494599 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-01-077 Dear Ms. Troedsson: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Mary Lou Holt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' QUESTIONS 1. May Mayor Holt participate in the review of the floodwall project? 2. May Mayor Holt participate in the review of the plans to remodel the community hal1? 3. May Mayor Holt participate in ongoing management decisions for the community hall? CONCLUSIONS 1. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in a decision to approve the floodwall project. Since she has a conflict of interest in this decision, she may not participate in a review of this project. I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections I 8 I 09- I 8996, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-01-077 Page No. 2 2. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in a decision to remodel and expand the community hall and may not participate in that decision. 3. Mayor Holt is presumed to have a conflict of interest in any decision relating to the ur. *d manag".Lnt of the community hall, unless the "public generally" exception appties. You have not provided suffrcient information for us to reach a conclusion on this issue. FACTS The Town of Yountville is a small town in the Napa Valley. The town encompasses an area ofjust 1.56 square miles and, as of January l, 2000, had a population of 3,768. The town is considering the erection of a floodwall that would protect two mobile home parks located at the end of the town from future flooding. Mayor Holt's principal residence is within 500 feet of the floodwall. However, her properly is not in the flood zone and will not be benefited by flood protection from the wall. The Yountville town engineer has estimated that approximately 70 percent of the homeowners in the town also will not receive a flood protection benefit from this project. Additionally, the town council will soon be reviewing a project to remodel and expand the community hall, a public building. There are also ongoing issues of use and -unug".n.nt of this public building that come before the council from time to time. The mayor's principal residence is within 500 feet of the corner of this property. Approximat ely 2.5 percent of all homeowners are within the same distance of the community center and may be affected by increased trafiic, parking, or noise as a result of the remodeling and expansion of the community hall. If you consider access to the facilities, the effect on Mayor Holt would be indistinguishable from 100 percent of the homeowners or property
